findings.summary: infrastructure/core/multi_project.py imports infrastructure/reporting at call-time, creating a downward dependency from core into a higher-level layer
evidence: Lines 170-174: `from infrastructure.reporting import collect_output_statistics, generate_pipeline_report, save_pipeline_report` inside a try block within _execute_multi_project_pipeline
Line 310: `from infrastructure.reporting import generate_multi_project_report` inside _run_executive_reporting
core/ is explicitly documented as the foundation layer (Layer 1); reporting/ consumes core/, so this import reverses the declared dependency direction
findings.suggestion: Move the reporting invocations out of the core orchestrator into the call-site (scripts/execute_multi_project.py or a dedicated orchestration module above core/) so core/ never imports from reporting/.

findings.summary: StageResult is defined independently in both infrastructure/core/checkpoint.py and infrastructure/reporting/pipeline_reporter.py with overlapping but inconsistent fields
evidence: checkpoint.py StageResult fields: name, exit_code, duration, timestamp, completed
pipeline_reporter.py StageResult fields: name, exit_code, duration, status, output_files, errors, warnings
Both are imported independently; callers must know which StageResult they hold, and the two cannot be substituted for each other
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single StageResult in infrastructure/core/checkpoint.py (or a shared models module). Add the fields the reporter needs (status, errors) to the canonical class and delete the duplicate.

findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py uses importlib.util.exec_module at import time to load a script file, injecting it into sys.modules — a runtime side-effect that violates module boundary clarity
evidence: Lines 117-122: spec_from_file_location / module_from_spec / exec_module used to load 06_llm_review.py and register it as 'scripts.llm_review' in sys.modules at package import time
This makes the scripts package's import side-effectful and couples the package init to a numbered script file whose path is hardcoded
findings.suggestion: Move the ReviewMode enum and main() function that callers need into an infrastructure module (e.g. infrastructure/llm/review/runner.py) and import from there directly, eliminating the dynamic exec_module pattern.

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py contains no business logic of its own; it only re-exports from sibling submodules plus one function from infrastructure/core/file_operations.py, creating an unnecessary indirection layer
evidence: Lines 24-48: all public symbols are imported with noqa:F401 from citations, metadata, models, platforms submodules
Lines 51-98: create_publication_package is the only function with a body, and it merely calls calculate_file_hash imported from infrastructure/core/file_operations.py
The file exists primarily to satisfy infrastructure/publishing/__init__.py's re-export surface, not to implement any cohesive responsibility
findings.suggestion: Either fold create_publication_package into the appropriate submodule (e.g. metadata.py) and have publishing/__init__.py import directly from submodules, or give core.py a genuine responsibility boundary. The current shape blurs which module owns which behavior.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/config_loader.py names its module-level logger `_logger` (private-prefixed) while every other module in infrastructure/core/ uses the public name `logger`
evidence: config_loader.py line 17: `_logger = get_logger(__name__)`
All 14 other infrastructure/core/ modules with a module-level logger use `logger = get_logger(__name__)` (file_operations, pipeline, multi_project, checkpoint, environment, cli, etc.)
findings.suggestion: Rename `_logger` to `logger` in config_loader.py to match the convention used across every sibling module.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/__init__.py exports two different callables both named monitor_performance — one context manager from performance.py and one decorator from performance_monitor.py — and resolves the collision by aliasing only the second, leaving callers of `from infrastructure.core import monitor_performance` silently getting the context manager
evidence: __init__.py line 112: `monitor_performance` imported from .performance (context manager)
__init__.py line 116: `from .performance_monitor import monitor_performance as monitor_performance_decorator`

The first import is overwritten in-namespace if both are live, and the second is aliased to a different name — the __all__ list at line 195-199 lists both 'monitor_performance' and 'monitor_performance_decorator', creating an invisible naming collision for callers who use positional star-imports or rely on the unaliased name
findings.suggestion: Give each function a unique, intention-revealing name from the start: rename performance.py's version to `performance_context` and performance_monitor.py's version to `profile_function`. Export both under their distinct names.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core functions inconsistently mix return-False/None on failure with raising typed TemplateError subclasses, with no documented per-function contract, making call-site handling unpredictable
evidence: clean_output_directory returns False on PermissionError/OSError but does not raise
clean_root_output_directory returns False on bare except Exception
clean_coverage_files returns False on bare except Exception
copy_final_deliverables returns a stats dict with errors list instead of raising
MissingDependencyError.__init__ raises typed exceptions elsewhere in exceptions.py

infrastructure/core/__init__.py docstring acknowledges the two-tier contract but the actual implementations are inconsistent: file_operations.py functions never raise TemplateError subclasses even for truly unexpected failures like shutil.rmtree on a corrupt FS
findings.suggestion: Establish a clear rule per class of failure: expected-absence conditions return bool/None; unexpected environment failures raise the appropriate TemplateError subclass. Audit file_operations.py to raise FileOperationError instead of returning False for PermissionError and unexpected OSError cases.

findings.summary: calculate_file_hash silently swallows all exceptions and returns None, losing error context entirely
evidence: Lines 582-588: bare `except Exception: return None` with no logging, no re-raise, and no indication to the caller of what went wrong (wrong algorithm name, I/O error, permission error are all indistinguishable)
findings.suggestion: At minimum log the exception at DEBUG level before returning None so errors are traceable. For unexpected failures (bad algorithm name, permission error) consider raising FileOperationError to match the module's stated error contract.

findings.summary: The codebase maintains two parallel error vocabularies — typed TemplateError subclasses (exceptions.py) and string-formatting InfraError constants (errors.py) — with no consistent rule for when each is used, forcing callers to handle both surfaces
evidence: pipeline.py uses InfraError constants (PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED, STAGE_EXCEPTION) to format log messages but does not raise them as exceptions
cli.py uses InfraError constants (CLI_COMMAND_FAILED, PIPELINE_EXECUTION_FAILED) the same way
exceptions.py PipelineError, BuildError, ScriptExecutionError are the typed hierarchy for the same domain
There is no module-level policy stating InfraError is for logging strings and TemplateError is for raising; a reader must infer it by observation
findings.suggestion: Document explicitly in infrastructure/core/__init__.py (or a dedicated errors.md) that InfraError constants are logging-only message formatters and TemplateError subclasses are the raise-able hierarchy. Consider whether InfraError constants should carry a corresponding exception_class field to make the mapping explicit.

findings.summary: MissingDependencyError.__init__ performs `import platform` and `import shutil` inside the exception constructor body, causing import-time side effects inside an error path
evidence: Lines 422-426: `import platform` and `import shutil` are called inside __init__ of MissingDependencyError, inside the branch `if recovery_commands is None and dependency:`
These are standard library modules and will always succeed, but executing imports inside exception constructors is unconventional and can mask circular import issues during error handling
findings.suggestion: Move `import platform` and `import shutil` to the module top-level in exceptions.py. They are stdlib modules with no side effects and can safely be top-level imports.
